text: The robotic right hemicolectomy is performed using the da Vinci robot system, with complete mesocolic excision and endocyanine green-guided intracorporeal anastomosis for a cecal adenocarcinoma in a thirty-eight-year-old male patient.

The robotic right hemicolectomy is performed using the da Vinci robot system, with complete mesocolic excision and endocyanine green-guided intracorporeal anastomosis for a cecal adenocarcinoma. The surgical field shows precise robotic manipulation of the colon and surrounding tissues.
The patient is positioned in a thirty-degree Trendelenburg position. A pneumoperitoneum is established at twelve millimeters of mercury using a Veress needle.
The patient is positioned in a thirty-degree Trendelenburg position, and pneumoperitoneum is established at twelve millimeters of mercury using a Veress needle.

Under laparoscopic vision, a 12mm port is placed in the left lumbar region for exploration of the abdominal cavity, confirming the absence of liver metastasis or peritoneal carcinosis. Four 8mm robotic trocars are inserted along a line from the left upper quadrant to the hypogastric region, followed by docking of the robotic system.

A 12mm laparoscopic port is placed under direct vision in the left lumbar region for exploration of the abdominal cavity, confirming the absence of liver metastasis or peritoneal carcinosis. Four 8mm robotic trocars are inserted along a line from the left upper quadrant to the hypogastric region, followed by docking of the robotic system.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum of the mesentery inferior to the ileocolic vessels using monopolar forceps. A blunt dissection is performed to develop the avascular plane between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum. Lymphatic tissue at the ileomesenteric junctions and along the right side of the superior mesenteric vein is completely resected.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum of the mesentery just below the ileocolic vessels using monopolar forceps. A blunt dissection is performed to develop the avascular plane between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum. Lymphatic tissue at the ileomesenteric junctions and along the right side of the superior mesenteric vein is completely resected.

The fourth robotic arm retracts tissue using a fenestrated grasper, while the assistant applies countertraction with a laparoscopic instrument. Fenestrated bipolar forceps and monopolar curved scissors are utilized for precise dissection.

The fourth robotic arm retracts tissue using a fenestrated grasper, while the assistant applies countertraction with a laparoscopic instrument. Fenestrated bipolar forceps and monopolar curved scissors are used for precise dissection.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic artery and divides it using clips and a bipolar vessel sealing device. Subsequently, the ileocolic vein is isolated and dissected in a similar manner.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic artery and divides it using clips and a bipolar vessel sealing device. Subsequently, the ileocolic vein is isolated and dissected using the same technique.

The surgeon continues the mesocolic plane dissection cranially towards the C-loop of the duodenum, exposing the pancreatic head. The right colic pedicle is isolated and dissected, with complete dissection along the avascular plane over the preduodenal pancreatic fascia.

The surgeon continues the mesocolic plane dissection cranially, exposing the C-loop of the duodenum and the pancreatic head. The right colic pedicle is isolated and dissected, with complete dissection conducted over the preduodenal pancreatic fascia along the avascular plane.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament, reaching and mobilizing the hepatic flexure. The transverse mesocolon and the right branch of the middle colic artery are transected, preparing the colon for further transection.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament, reaching and mobilizing the hepatic flexure. The transverse mesocolon and the right branch of the middle colic artery are transected, preparing the colon for transection.

The surgeon completes dissection over the girotifacia, carefully preserving the right testicular vessels and ureter. The tumor adheres to the peritoneum in the right iliac region, necessitating en bloc mobilization of the cecum with the parietal peritoneum, including the serosa of the right side of the bladder dome.

The surgeon completes the dissection over the girotifacia, carefully preserving the right testicular vessels and ureter. The tumor is adhered to the peritoneum of the right iliac region, necessitating an en bloc dissection of the cecum with the parietal peritoneum, including the serosa of the right side of the bladder dome.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative methylene blue dye test to confirm the integrity of the bladder wall, showing no neoplastic invasion into the muscular layer.

The surgeon assesses the bladder wall, confirming that neoplastic invasion does not involve the muscular layer. An intraoperative methylene blue dye test is performed, visually confirming the integrity of the bladder following dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions tethering the ileum to the retroperitoneum, allowing for complete mobility of the bowel.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions tethering the ileum to the retroperitoneum, facilitating the complete mobility of the bowel.

The surgeon uses a robotic linear cutting stapler to transect the transverse colon and terminal ileum. Endocyanine green fluorescence angiography is performed, visually confirming the perfusion and viability of the colon.

The surgeon uses a robotic linear cutting stapler to transect the transverse colon and terminal ileum. Endocyanine green fluorescence angiography is performed, visually confirming the perfusion and viability of the colon.

The specimen is detached and positioned over the right liver to increase working space. The transverse colon and ileum are aligned side-to-side, and enterotomies are created along the anti-mesenteric border. The surgeon inserts a linear robotic cutting stapler through the enterotomies to perform the intracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis, followed by a robotic hand-sewn running suture to close the enterotomy.

The surgical specimen is placed over the right liver to increase working space. The transverse colon and ileum are aligned side-to-side, and enterotomies are created along the anti-mesenteric border. A linear robotic cutting stapler is inserted through the enterotomies to perform the intracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis, followed by a robotic hand-sewn running suture to close the enterotomy.
The surgeon creates a second seromuscular layer using single stitches. The sovrubic trocar wound is extended to facilitate the extraction of the specimen.
The surgeon creates a second seromuscular layer using single stitches. The sovrubic trocar wound is extended to facilitate the extraction of the specimen from the abdominal cavity.
